data.title: Dick Van Dyke turns 100: 5 things to know about the Mary Poppins star
data.description:
Dick Van Dyke turns 100 today, on December 13, and the world is celebrating this milestone with him. The Mary Poppins icon and author of 100 Rules for Living to 100: An Optimist’s Guide to a Happy Life recently admitted to The New York Times that while he’s “super old ... sometimes I feel like I’m 15 again”.

The secret to ageing gracefully, Van Dyke says, is exercise, pets and playfulness. In a chat with Today’s Al Roker, the Emmy-winning actor also shared that his 54-year-old wife, Arlene Silver, “keeps [him] a teenager”. And, speaking of his legacy, the Disney legend mused, “The movies and the TV show [The Dick Van Dyke Show] kind of speak for me.”
Here are five interesting facts about the star.
His parents weren’t in showbiz

Dick Van Dyke was born in West Plains, Missouri, to Loren “Cookie” Van Dyke and Hazel Van Dyke. His father was a minor-league baseball player and musician who became a salesman to support his family, he told The Guardian. His mother was a stenographer. His younger brother, Jerry Van Dyke, was a comedian.
Before becoming an actor, Van Dyke served as a radio announcer in the US Army Air Corps during the Second World War and briefly ran an ad agency, according to CNN.
He once didn’t know his real birthday

In his memoir My Lucky Life In and Out of Show Business, Van Dyke revealed that just before turning 18, he was contemplating joining the US Air Force. That’s when his mother told him he was actually already 18 and that his date of birth had been pushed forward from December to March because he was born premature. However, his grandmother later disclosed that the date was altered because he was born out of wedlock – a scandal in 1925 and especially in their small town.
His was close with Walt Disney

During Van Dyke’s recent Vandy High Tea charity event, the Chitty Chitty Bang Bang star opened up about being “the last living person who knew the real Walt Disney”. He told guests, “We decided that emotionally we were both 13 years old,” adding, “He was a big kid, just so full of enthusiasm, and I just loved him.”
He gave up drinking and smoking in his 50s

While reminiscing about Disney, Van Dyke also explained that he had once been a heavy smoker. The centenarian then revealed that he gave up cigarettes and alcohol in his fifties when he realised he had an “addictive personality”. He sought treatment for his alcoholism, per People, but admitted that quitting smoking was “twice as hard”.
Regrets? He has a few

Van Dyke also told Al Roker that he regrets turning down the offer to play James Bond after Sean Connery. At the time, he felt he’d already established himself in a certain type of film and that audiences wouldn’t appreciate him taking on the role.
He also revealed during the Vandy High Tea that he regrets passing on projects with Cary Grant and Sophia Loren, the latter because his agent turned the opportunity down. The Vantastix a capella group performer “never forgave” him for the lapse.

data.title: China’s green tech revolution will outlast geopolitics with scale and cost: Arif Aga
data.description:
The global green energy transition is less about geopolitics and more about economic reality, according to Arif Aga, director of global renewable engineering consultancy SgurrEnergy.
For many nations seeking to adopt clean energy, their priority remains the practical pursuit of lowering costs, a reality that has entrenched China’s leading role in the global clean power supply.
According to the renewable energy technical adviser, the vast scale of Chinese manufacturing and its rapid enhancement of technologies are the catalysts for the industry’s dramatic cost reductions.
“Geopolitics is a temporary thing. It is all about politics and politicians. Eventually things will be different,” Aga told the Post in an interview this month.
“If any country has to scale up its renewable energy penetration to lower the carbon footprint, they have to have a dependency on a partner who has a long track record that would ensure projects are durable,” he said.
“Technological changes are happening at a very high pace. An established player will have an upper edge to meet the technological change requirements or embed the technological change design to the system.”
“With the scales going up, China definitely is here to stay for a long, long time,” he added.
Aga, who leads the India-headquartered company, pointed to China’s dominance in the renewable supply chain – from raw materials to new innovations.


“We have worked in more than 55 countries and out of them, Chinese modules and components are being used in 52 countries – be it the United States, Africa, Southeast Asia, India and the Middle East,” he said.
Describing some of these renewable projects as “shipped by China, executed in Southeast Asia”, Aga said his firm had taken part in initial projects in countries such as Malaysia, Indonesia, Vietnam and the Philippines.
Southeast Asian countries have seen their renewable energy capacity more than double over the last decade. But they also need to modernise electricity grids to sustain their economic boom and unlock the full potential of renewable energy, according to independent global energy think tank Ember.
The region was also the largest photovoltaic (PV) production hub making solar panels outside China in 2024, with direct investments from large Chinese manufacturers speeding up the expansion of manufacturing capacity, according to the International Energy Agency.
“These investments were primarily intended to geographically diversify solar PV manufacturing to serve markets with trade restrictions on Chinese imports, particularly the United States,” the agency said in its annual renewables report released in October.
In addition to designing and engineering projects including solar, wind and energy storage, Aga said the consultancy also inspected component factories, which gave him first-hand experience working with Chinese PV module manufacturers.
He said around 60 per cent of the firm’s inspection services were conducted in Chinese factories and 35 per cent in India.
“Chinese participation in renewable energy has been remarkable,” Aga said. “The edge that Chinese companies have [for example, with] PV modules is the scalability.”

“There is a lot of feedback that we as consultants give for technological enhancements to reduce costs. The way Chinese companies pick up or accept our feedback and execute it – I do not see any other country doing that,” he said.
He explained that the rapid adoption of technological enhancements was linked to the sheer size of the Chinese manufacturing sector.
For instance, a manufacturer producing 50 gigawatts (GW) of modules will have a research and development budget proportional to its larger production capacity and revenue, compared to a 5GW company, allowing it to invest significantly more in innovation, he said.
“Chinese companies have that upper edge because of scalability and their larger revenues to do all kinds of enhancements. That is commendable,” Aga added.
He said lowering costs had always been the key priority for green energy projects, pointing to the potential for Southeast Asia to adopt more floating solar plants, given their vast areas of water, to cut costs.
“Implementation cost across the geography has to be lower than the conventional [energy sources], only then it would support energy transition,” Aga said.
“We as global citizens have to look at saving our planet and making the planet green. That is what we all want, but at what cost? Eventually, everything boils down to the cost.
“Unless and until it is commercially viable, banks are not going to fund the project. Developers, irrespective of how big [they are], they would eventually like to make money.”

On the flip side of the economic coin, the International Energy Agency warned about the “financial sustainability of equipment manufacturers” in its report released in October.
“Major solar PV and wind manufacturers have reported large losses despite surging global installations,” it said.
“In China, solar PV prices are down over 60 per cent since 2023 due to supply glut of modules and competition for market share. This has reduced the margins of the largest manufacturers to -10 per cent, with cumulative losses reaching almost US$5 billion since the beginning of 2024.”
It said continued growth in overcapacity had driven PV module prices to record lows in 2024.
According to the IEA, the average annual global wholesale price saw a decline of almost 45 per cent year on year to US$0.09/W. This price stabilised at that level in the first half of 2025.
“This price is below the production cost for most manufacturers, including leading Chinese firms, and is widely considered unsustainable in the medium to long term,” it said, adding that net profit margins of major PV manufacturers became negative in 2024 and early 2025.
Trade restrictions also meant actual PV module prices varied across countries. US-imported modules cost three times the global average, while India-produced modules were 70 per cent higher.
The issue was also raised in February by research firm BloombergNEF which noted: “China’s clean technology manufacturing overcapacity has led to rising protectionism in the form of import tariffs by countries to avoid cheap imports upending their own energy markets.”

data.title: Primary school near Hong Kong estate destroyed in deadly blaze hopes to move
data.description:
The principal of a Hong Kong primary school opposite the scene of the deadly Tai Po fire has expressed hope that the institution could relocate, saying the horrifying view of the charred buildings will put parents off and affect enrolment.
Tai Po Baptist Public School head Siu Ting said the campus was still sealed off and there was currently no date set for when pupils might return.
The school’s students had been learning in makeshift classrooms at two public universities for the past two weeks and will attend classes at two other primary schools starting from next Monday and until at least the end of the school year.
Siu said she doubted that Tai Po Baptist Public School’s campus near Wang Fuk Court was still fit for educational purposes.
“Going back to our campus to attend classes is not an ideal arrangement for every stakeholder of the school,” Siu said.
School supervisor Tsang Ka-shek earlier said the nearby scene was too disturbing.
The school, which is just opposite the estate, is the only institution to remain cordoned off after the deadly inferno that broke out on November 26.

The fire left at least 160 people dead and another 79 injured after raging through seven of the housing complex’s eight blocks for about 43 hours.
Siu added that since it was still unclear whether Wang Fuk Court would be demolished and rebuilt, the uncertainty gave school management additional concerns about letting students return to the campus.
“We very much hope our school can be relocated,” she said.
The principal said some parents had already said they would switch to other schools if the students were told to return to the campus.
Siu said the school would face further challenges in January when parents would make their choices using the central allocation system to determine where their children would enrol for Primary One.
She said she hoped the government could provide a discretionary arrangement to the public school, given that such institutions were required to reach an enrolment threshold to receive funding or potentially face being axed.
The school’s wish to relocate elsewhere within Tai Po district was being conveyed to education authorities, she said.
“Our supervisor and incorporated management committee are still communicating this issue with the Education Bureau, but there is no magic. We have to wait,” Siu said.
The principal said the temporary relocation to two other primary schools could also pose challenges and expressed concerns that the presence of the additional students might affect others and create conflicts.

Tai Po Baptist Public School’s Primary Two and Four pupils, comprising eight classes, will move to NTWJWA Christian Remembrance of Grace Primary School.
The remaining pupils, comprising 18 classes, will go to Tai Po Government Primary School.
“We are not moving to vacant school premises. The schools where we are going to have students studying. Some of our teachers described it as lodging at someone else’s place,” Siu said.
“It is not only an issue of having a classroom. Students also have to get out of the classrooms and interact with others.
“We are afraid there will be conflicts among students and teachers. It will eventually affect students’ learning.”
Carol Chan Suk-yee, Tai Po Baptist Public School’s vice-principal, also said staff had not dared to bring too many things from the old location since they were using other institutions’ campuses.
The Education Bureau earlier said it would give HK$5 million (US$642,600) to the three schools affected by the fire to support them during the transition period.
Siu said the funds had been used to set up new offices at the two other schools and to renovate some rooms to suit Tai Po Baptist Public School’s needs.
In a reply to the Post, the bureau said it would assess the situation and consider all feasible options to help the school with its campus issues as soon as possible, adding that students’ interests were education authorities’ primary concern.
“[The bureau] will announce the arrangements in due course … and continue to maintain close contact with the school and provide necessary assistance,” a spokeswoman said.

data.title: Fire breaks out at flat in Hong Kong’s Tin Shui Wai, 1 injured and 200 evacuated
data.description:
A fire broke out at a flat in a public housing estate in Hong Kong’s Tin Shui Wai early on Saturday, leaving a man severely injured, with more than 200 residents evacuated.
Authorities received multiple calls at 3.43am on Saturday from residents reporting the blaze at Tin Chak Estate in Tin Shui Wai, with occasional sounds of explosions.
Firefighters rushed to the scene and rescued a man living in the flat on fire. He was found conscious but later fell into a coma after being rushed to Tuen Mun Hospital. He sustained burns on 20 per cent of his skin.
A total of 11 men and 12 women were evacuated by firefighters, while more than 200 others left their flats of their own accord.

The blaze was subsequently extinguished and residents returned to their flats.
The Fire Services Department said it was still investigating the cause of the blaze.

data.title: North Korea’s Kim reveals soldiers cleared mines for Russia, in rare public statement
data.description:
North Korea sent troops to clear mines in Russia’s Kursk region earlier this year, leader Kim Jong-un said in a speech carried on Saturday by state media, in a rare acknowledgement of the deadly tasks assigned to its soldiers there.
Pyongyang has sent thousands of troops to fight for Moscow as it presses ahead with its nearly four-year invasion of Ukraine, according to South Korean and Western intelligence agencies.
Analysts say Russia is giving North Korea financial aid, military technology, food and energy supplies in return, allowing the diplomatically isolated nation to sidestep tough international sanctions on its nuclear and missile programmes.
Last month, Russia’s Defence Ministry said North Korean troops who helped Russia repel a major Ukrainian incursion into the Kursk region were now playing an important role in clearing the area of mines.

Under a mutual defence pact between the two countries, North Korea last year sent some 14,000 soldiers to fight alongside Russia in Kursk, and more than 6,000 were killed.
Hailing the return of an engineering regiment, Kim said that they wrote “letters to their hometowns and villages at breaks of the mine-clearing hours”, according to the Korean Central News Agency (KCNA).
The regiment suffered the “heart-rending loss of nine lives” during the 120-day deployment that started in August, Kim said in a speech at a welcome ceremony on Friday, KCNA reported.
He awarded the deceased soldiers state honours to “add eternal lustre” to their bravery.
“All of you, both officers and soldiers, displayed mass heroism overcoming unimaginable mental and physical burdens almost every day,” Kim said.


The troops had been able to “work a miracle of turning a vast area of danger zone into a safe and secure one in a matter of less than three months”.
Saying that the regiment had showed “absolute loyalty” to the party and the state, Kim praised the troops’ political indoctrination, discipline and unity, calling their performance a model for the armed forces.
North Korea only confirmed in April that it had deployed troops to support Russia and that its soldiers had been killed in combat.
Images released by KCNA showed a smiling Kim embracing returned soldiers, some in wheelchairs, at the grand ceremony in Pyongyang on Friday.
Additional reporting by Reuters

data.title: The case for separating the Dutch and Chinese arms of Nexperia
data.description:
Feel strongly about these letters, or any other aspects of the news? Share your views by emailing us your Letter to the Editor at letters@scmp.com or filling in this Google form. Submissions should not exceed 400 words
The Dutch government’s recent decision to suspend its use of the Goods Availability Act against Nexperia has been described as a de-escalation, but the underlying issue remains unresolved. The court-appointed administrator still controls the company’s voting rights, and the chief executive appointed by Nexperia’s parent company, Wingtech, has not been reinstated. In practice, an approved acquisition remains under long-term external control with no clear path back to normal governance.
Europe has already established a precedent for such situations. In 2022, the United Kingdom required Nexperia to divest its Newport wafer plant under the National Security and Investment Act, a measure structured as a forced sale rather than a confiscation. When national security laws block foreign ownership, divestment – not indefinite custodianship – is the recognised remedy.
China should apply the same principle to the Nexperia operations in Guangdong. Spinning off the plant into an entity governed entirely under Chinese law would mirror Europe’s approach and create a clean separation between the European and Chinese portions of Nexperia. No dramatic statement or retaliatory framing is needed; this approach merely applies Europe’s new logic to Asia. Nexperia Europe is now functionally divorced from its Chinese parent, and Dutch authorities have shown no interest in restoring the status quo ante.
To demonstrate restraint and maintain international credibility, China could place the proceeds of the Guangdong divestment in escrow on behalf of Wingtech. This would show that China is following a transparent, commercially recognisable process while ensuring the parent company is not deprived of its economic rights. The escrow mechanism would also counter any claim that China was nationalising assets; it would align with the structure Europe used in the Newport case.
A clean spin-off would stabilise the situation for all parties. Europe would retain its Dutch and British operations under governance structures it considers safe. China would preserve the operational integrity of the Guangdong facilities, supplied largely by domestic wafer manufacturers and serving both Chinese and international customers. The global semiconductor supply chain would benefit from clarity: two parallel entities, operating independently, each subject to its own jurisdiction rather than forced into a contested hybrid.
A prolonged limbo satisfies no one. A structured, symmetrical solution offers a way out for both sides. If Europe regards Nexperia Europe as a strategic asset, it should buy out Wingtech’s stake in the non-Chinese asset. China, for its part, should spin off the Guangdong facility. This would not be a hostile act, but a path to stability in a sector where uncertainty carries a global cost.
Erik Cota-Robles, Helsinki, Finland
Malaysian police are duty-bound to take reports
Police officers in Malacca, Malaysia this week barred a mother and her daughter from entering their police station to report a car accident because they were wearing skirts that were deemed too short. In your report, human rights lawyer Rajesh Nagarajan called the police action a violation of constitutional rights. I am similarly troubled.
The law is clear on the duty of the police to take reports. Section 107(4) of the Criminal Procedure Code states that a police officer is duty bound to receive any information in relation to any offence committed in Malaysia. Information relating to a car accident could be information relating to an offence.
Further, section 107(3)(a) states that the information given by a person relating to the commission of an offence to a police officer, who at the time of receiving the information is not in a police station, is deemed to be received at a police station. Accordingly, a police report need not be made in a police station. It can be made anywhere as long as it is made to a police officer.
So, if a member of the public can’t step into a police station to make a report for whatever reason, the police must step out of the station to take the report.
Mohamad Hafiz Hassan, lecturer, Faculty of Law, Multimedia University
Heavy penalties needed to stop smoking on work sites
It is a relief to learn from your editorial that the government is very likely going to ban smoking outright on construction and renovation sites.
The recent tragic Tai Po fire may or may not have been caused by a carelessly discarded cigarette butt. However, whatever the findings of the inquiry into the fire’s causes, it still seems wise to ban smoking on these sites, as some companies have done. The human price for any accident that could occur is too great.
What I am concerned about – if a law is enacted – are the proposed penalties for disobeying the law – i.e. the on-the-spot fines. I doubt whether an addicted smoker will be deterred by a financial penalty.
May I respectfully suggest that the penalty, in addition to a financial one, be immediate expulsion from the site, coupled with a ban on working on such sites for a certain amount of time. The danger from carelessly discarded cigarette butts is too high to allow smokers who cannot control their habit anywhere near a construction or renovation site.
Chris Stubbs, Discovery Bay

data.title: East Asia Super League agrees multi-year deal with Under Armour for players and teams
data.description:
The East Asia Super League (EASL) announced a multi-year clothing and footwear deal with Under Armour for players and teams on Friday.
The 12-team basketball league features Hong Kong Eastern, who are playing in the EASL for the second year of a two-year deal this season.
Eastern won half of their six group matches last season to finish third and missed out on the semi-finals in Macau.
The league’s partnership with Under Armour also extends into EASL Future Champions, the league’s youth development programme across Asia.
“Partnering with a globally recognised brand like Under Armour is an incredibly important step in EASL’s growth,” Henry Kerins, the league’s CEO, said.
“Their success and worldwide reputation strengthen our ability to elevate Asian basketball, and in return we will use our international platform to champion their brand throughout the region. Together, we are pushing each other forward and raising the standard for the sport.”

The agreement also includes collaborative fan engagement programmes, regional grass-roots projects and special edition product collaborations that celebrate the spirit of Asian basketball.
“We are a brand rooted in team sports, the objective is to align the brand’s performance capabilities with the growth of the sport and the fostering of next-generation talents,” Simon Pestridge, Under Armour’s managing director in the Asia-Pacific region, said.
“The partnership with EASL is a strategic step to support the region’s developing basketball ecosystem and also reflects Under Armour’s focus on advancing basketball across Asia and expanding opportunities for athletes at all levels.”

data.title: From Singapore to Taiwan, Japan must face its past for Asia’s future
data.description:
The Imperial Japanese Navy destroyers Shigure, Isokaze, Hamakaze and Yukizake were assigned to escort duties when convoys were sent to Singapore to collect badly needed supplies for Japan during the second world war. The destroyers protected the light carrier Ryuho carrying a load of aircraft bound for the Japanese colony of Taiwan.
This brief synopsis appears in episode six of the second series of the Japanese anime Kantai Collection (“Fleet Girls Collection”), also known as KanColle, in which Imperial Japanese warships are reimagined as teenage girls fighting an unnamed, faceless evil. In this retelling, they are heroines. This episode aired as recently as February 2023.

There is no apparent awareness that the Japanese occupation of Singapore, from February 1942 to September 1945, was anything but benign. “Collecting supplies” from Singapore meant the ruthless extraction of resources from conquered Southeast Asia, with romusha (forced labourers) taken from what is now Indonesia by the Imperial Japanese Army and left to die on the streets of Singapore when they could no longer work.
That such an anime could air in 2023 is a telling symptom of the lack of historical awareness within Japanese society regarding the atrocities inflicted by the country’s forces during the war. For those who need more evidence, a visit to the museum at the Yasukuni Shrine will be instructive.

Now there is a new furore caused by Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi’s remarks on Taiwan, which were interpreted to imply that Japan might intervene militarily in the event of an armed conflict.
For Beijing, Taiwan is a particularly sensitive matter. It was the last territory seized from the moribund Qing dynasty during the last decade of the 19th century, the so-called century of humiliation. Taiwan was ruled by Japan after its conquest in 1895.
Troops from Taiwan served as part of Japanese garrisons throughout Southeast Asia. In Syonan (wartime Singapore) Hokkien-speaking detectives from Taiwan formed part of the internal security apparatus enforcing Japanese rule, often with great brutality towards the Chinese and Eurasian population.
At the end of the second world war in 1945, Taiwan was returned to China, which had been fighting Japanese aggression since 1931.
Taiwan’s secession is a matter of vital national interest for Beijing; it would lead to war. The idea that Japanese military forces could be involved in another war with China is disturbing to many, not just in Northeast Asia.
It is for the aggressors of yesteryear to be the peacemakers of tomorrow

In response to the current furore, some have expressed hope that China and Japan could find ways to resolve their differences and move on, just as Singapore and Southeast Asia have done. But can the past be whitewashed and the victims simply told to forget?
In contrast to Japan, Germany has made a point of not forgetting what was done by their forebears. Outside Berlin’s Wittenbergplatz U-Bahn station, right next to the city’s largest department store, stands a memorial listing 12 death camps, bearing the inscription, “Locations of the Horror; that we must never forget.” On December 7, 1970, during a state visit to Poland, Chancellor Willy Brandt went down on his knees at the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising memorial in a gesture of contrition for the massacre of the Jewish population committed by the Germans during the war.
In Germany, the display of Nazi symbols is prohibited. No vessel of the modern-day Bundesmarine bears the names of warships from the second world war. By contrast, the Japan Maritime Self-Defence Force operates the JS Kaga, classed as a multipurpose destroyer but now effectively a light aircraft carrier following upgrades. Its namesake, the IJNS Kaga, was one of the six fleet carriers that attacked Pearl Harbour in 1941. Both ships fly the same naval ensign, unchanged since the second world war.
An anime like Fleet Girls portraying ships of the second world war-era German Kriegsmarine as teenaged girls escorting convoys bringing resources to Germany plundered from conquered territories would provoke an enormous outcry, foremost in Germany as well as all over Europe.

The reconciliation between France and Germany formed the bedrock of the European Union. This was graphically illustrated by the picture of German Chancellor Helmut Kohl and French President Francois Mitterrand holding hands on September 22, 1984 in Verdun, where one of the bloodiest battles of the first world war was fought. A plaque was installed in front of the Douaumont Ossuary with the inscription: “We have reconciled. We have come to understand each other. We have become friends.”
Lasting peace in Asia can only come if there is a similar reconciliation between Japan and China, and between Japan and its former colony, Korea. Perhaps a wiser Japanese prime minister might make a gesture like that of German Chancellor Willy Brandt at the Warsaw Ghetto Memorial. Bowing in the Memorial Hall of the Victims of the Nanking massacre in modern-day Nanjing comes to mind, as a clear sign of contrition for the sins of the past.
One obstacle stands in the way of any such reconciliation, however. The current tension between Beijing, Tokyo and Seoul suits Washington. The “threat” that China poses to America is not ideological; after the Soviet Union’s collapse, capitalism is triumphant even in nominally communist China. The challenge is economic.
An East Asian bloc comprising China, South Korea and Japan would be a formidable economic force. For anyone with the aim of perpetuating perpetual American dominance, this is not something that will be countenanced. Far better from the point of view of “America First” advocates that no such reconciliation should ever happen.

Nonetheless, one can only hope that wiser counsels will prevail and that peace through reconciliation in Asia will be seen as the paramount goal to pursue. The alternative may very well be blundering blindly into a war that no one wants. Such a conflict would be generational, like the one between France and Germany between 1870 and 1945. There will be no winners, least of all in Asia.
In China, September 18 is commemorated each year to mark the day in 1931 when the Imperial Japanese Army engineered the Mukden Incident as a pretext to invade and occupy the northeast provinces of China. The puppet state of Manchukuo was set up, with the last Qing emperor, Puyi, as its figurehead. This incident was the real start of the second world war, not the Pearl Harbour attack on December 7, 1941, as so many Westerners believe.
Next year will be the 95th anniversary of the Mukden Incident and the 85th anniversary of the attack on Pearl Harbour. That would be a good time for the process of reconciliation to start. It is for the aggressors of yesteryear to be the peacemakers of tomorrow. One hopes that they will have the wisdom and courage to do so.
Walter Woon is a former ambassador of Singapore to Germany and the European Union.

data.title: China’s copper heartland vows to build US$28 billion empire, dominate in global market
data.description:
A key industrial hub in eastern China is launching a push to transform itself into one of the world’s top copper-smelting bases, aiming to forge stronger supply chains for the strategic metal as prices flirt with record highs.
In an implementation plan released on Tuesday, the Shandong provincial government pledged to expand its copper industry, with the next two years seeing rapid advancements. The move comes as geopolitical tensions and the race for technological dominance shore up copper’s role as a critical asset.
“[We strive] to further enhance the resilience and security of the industrial supply chains,” the document stated. The plan envisions Shandong as “a globally competitive copper-smelting base” and a domestic leader in research and development for high-end copper materials.
If all goes to plan, the value of the provincial copper industry should surpass 200 billion yuan (US$28.35 billion) by 2027. The current industry’s size was not provided.
Shandong is already home to major players such as Yanggu Xiangguang Copper and CNMC Albetter Copper. The government vowed to “cultivate key enterprises” and support them to “lead on the national level in the copper-smelting sector”.

While China is the world’s top importer and consumer of copper, Shandong intends to expand the export market.
“We support qualified enterprises to ‘go global’ and cooperate with [member countries participating in] the Belt and Road Initiative,” the document said, referring to the central government’s years-long endeavour to link economies into a China-centred trading network.
Shandong authorities also encouraged cross-border mergers and acquisitions, equity participation and technological cooperation, “so as to deeply integrate into the global copper industry value chain”.
The push coincides with a surge in global prices. The three-month copper benchmark on the London Metal Exchange rose 0.48 per cent to US$11,611.50 a tonne on Thursday, and the base metal’s price has approached an all-time high of US$11,771, according to industry trade publication Offshore Engineer.
“Prices for copper and other industrial metals like aluminium and lithium rallied this year as interest rates declined, the US dollar weakened, and expectations for Chinese economic growth improved,” wrote research analyst Eoin Dinsmore at Goldman Sachs in his team’s report, issued Thursday.
“Supply disruptions, policy changes, and massive spending on artificial intelligence (AI) gave prices an extra boost,” he added, noting that the investment bank expects that a continued global surplus of supply would prevent copper prices from exceeding US$11,000 for a sustained period in 2026.
One factor that could lead to higher demand on the London copper market in 2026 is the potential for the US to place tariffs on refined copper imports, Dinsmore said.
“The US commerce secretary is expected to make a recommendation on copper tariffs to the White House by June 2026 (and possibly sooner),” he said. “Goldman Sachs Research’s base case is that a refined copper tariff of at least 25 per cent will be implemented shortly after.”
In Shandong, its initiative aligns with broader national goals. In February, 11 government departments, including the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology, released a plan aiming to increase China’s copper ore reserves by 5 to 10 per cent by 2027.

data.title: Towering traditions: Hong Kong’s centuries-old Kam Tin Jiao Festival returns
data.description:
Once every decade, villagers in Kam Tin build a nearly 30-metre (100-foot) tall bamboo structure – large enough to hold almost half the capacity of the Hong Kong Coliseum – to stage rituals and Cantonese opera performances for an audience of residents, deities and spirits alike.
The colossal structure, adorned with vibrant plaques and a flag, is the largest of its kind in Hong Kong and serves as the centrepiece of the HK$20 million (US$2.6 million) Jiao Festival organised by the indigenous Tang clan of Kam Tin. The five-day, six-night celebration will begin on Saturday evening.
“Throughout the generations, [this festival] reminds us that we must repay our gratitudes. It also unites our clan, as during every Jiao festival, everyone comes back,” said 66-year-old Tang Ho-nin, chairman of the Kam Tin Rural Committee and chief organiser of the festival.
Clansmen of the organising committee told the Post that the festival had been preserved in its original form for 340 years, out of respect for their ancestors and the desire to unite families across generations and across the world.
The Tang clan welcomed the government’s recent efforts to safeguard intangible cultural heritage, which they saw as an opportunity to share their festival with other residents in the city.
“We have been doing this [festival] all along, but now there’s a new name for it being intangible cultural heritage,” said Tang Wing-hong, 68, a village chief and member of the organising committee.
The festival features about 10 cultural practices recognised by authorities as intangible cultural heritage, including bamboo scaffolding, lion and dragon dancing, puppet theatre and the traditional cheongsams worn by villagers during rituals.
Being held shortly after last month’s deadly fire at Wang Fuk Court that killed at least 160 people, organisers have taken extra precautions to ensure safety.
Secretary of the organising committee Tang Hung-yan told the Post that additional meetings were held to allocate more manpower for night patrols around the festival, increase the number of fire extinguishers on site and enforce a strict no smoking policy.

A dedicated area will also be set up during the festival to appease the spirits of the Wang Fuk Court victims.
The estate had been covered in scaffolding and netting for renovation work since July last year. Investigations later found that flammable foam had been used to seal windows, and some of the netting samples failed fire-retardant tests.
Known in Chinese as “repayment for benevolence”, Kam Tin’s Jiao Festival dates back to 1685, when the Tang clansmen sought to thank two officials for allowing them to return to their homeland in Yuen Long.
The clan, along with other coastal communities, had been forced to move inland during the early years of the Qing dynasty (1644-1911) to prevent them from aiding forces led by Zheng Chenggong – a Japan-born, half-Chinese sea merchant who rose to power with his formidable maritime might from the coast of Fujian.
To commemorate the help of Zhou Youde, viceroy of Guangdong and Guangxi, and Wang Lairen, governor of Guangdong, villagers built the Chou Wong Yi Kung Study Hall in Shui Tau Tsuen, Kam Tin, and established the tradition of holding a Jiao Festival every 10 years.
According to cultural anthropologist Professor Liu Tik-sang of the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, Jiao festivals were common among village communities in the New Territories, serving as a way to thank the gods for their protection and to appease wandering spirits, preventing them from disturbing the living.
“These Jiao festivals were also a way for local leaders and elites to show their capabilities, while for villagers, it’s a chance to get protection from the gods and see friends they haven’t met in a long time,” Liu said.

What sets Kam Tin’s festival apart is its grand scale and its distinct Taoist tradition. Villagers invite ceremonial masters from a different Taoist school, resulting in unique ritual practices and interpretations.
Centred around five days and six nights of Taoist rituals from December 13 to 19, the festival’s ceremonies will be held at the bamboo scaffolding structure, and Taoist masters and family representatives will take part.
The rituals will culminate in a parade featuring a giant papier-mache warrior and the burning of offerings to appease spirits on the final evening, followed by a concluding procession returning the gods to the study hall on the next day.
After the ceremonies, the bamboo structure will be transformed into a 6,000-seat Cantonese opera stage. Besides villagers and visitors, the gods honoured and the ghosts appeased during the rituals are also counted among the audience.
Preparations begin 10 months in advance, with committee members arranging the construction of the scaffolding, commissioning papier-mache sculptures, selecting opera troupes and appointing heads of families to participate in the rituals.
Chairman Tang said this year’s festival would cost about HK$20 million (US$2.57 million), with HK$4.5 million spent on the scaffolding structure and HK$7 million on vegetarian poon choi – a communal banquet dish served in large layered bowls.
Tang Wai-tim, 61, a member of the committee’s reception and general affairs section, said organising the once-a-decade event typically fell to clansmen in their fifties and sixties, with elders passing on their knowledge and experience.
“Many of our clansmen return [after retirement] to take up duties in the village’s affairs. Our legacy begins bit by bit from here,” Tang, a former policeman, said.
Younger members were encouraged to enjoy the festivities and handle smaller tasks – such as carrying giant papier-mache figures during parades – before taking up larger roles later in life, he added.
